findings.issue: The public web OAuth handoff still accepts arbitrary external return_to URLs. /v1/oauth/web/init is on the public router and lets the caller supply both return_to and app_challenge; is_allowed_return_to ultimately logs and returns true for every URL that is not one of the small local/same-host fast paths. A malicious site can initiate a handoff with its own PKCE verifier/challenge, send the victim through the real provider consent flow, receive the redirected handoff_id/app_code on its external return_to, and redeem the victim's access token because PKCE only proves the attacker is the party that started the flow. This is not fixed by the remote-frontend callback sanitizer because attackers can call the public API directly.
findings.remediation: Reject unregistered external return_to values. Bind web handoffs to a trusted public_origin or to registered client_id/redirect_uri allow-list entries, and only allow arbitrary origins after explicit client registration that is checked again before redirecting and redeeming.

findings.issue: The post-login return_to sanitizer only checks same-origin, so it treats internal auth endpoints like /oauth/callback as safe. A crafted /login?return_to=/oauth/callback?error=access_denied flow can successfully redeem and store the access token, then immediately navigate back into OAuthCallbackPage after clearVerifier() has run; the second callback takes the error/missing-verifier branch, removes localStorage.access_token, and sends the user back to login. Same-origin is necessary for open-redirect safety, but it is not sufficient for callback re-entry safety.
findings.remediation: Constrain return_to to normal app destinations rather than every same-origin URL. Reject /oauth/callback, invitation completion callbacks, and other auth-processing routes (or maintain an explicit allow-list such as /nodes and /tasks) before calling window.location.assign(safeReturnTo).
